author: @mrdovie47
text: These printed heaters are used to keep lizards happy, but I've heard of someone building an heated cupboard with wire shelves for air flow for letting bread and baked goods rise with yeast. This is good for someone that does a lot of baking.

author: @boblordylordyhowie
text:
I know many professionals who seem to think rules and regulations are for "common folk" and not them. When I have pointed out the rules and regulations for selling anything electrical (50yrs an electrician from houses to substations, even MRI installations) they poo-poo it saying nobody will know but then when I tell them sticking a plug and a lampholder onto a bit of wood doesn't make them competent or the thing safe. They can use it in their own home but if they decide to make a business out of it, that is a completely different matter.
We used to have NVQ qualifications and us as apprentice trained electricians (4 years) called it No Valid Qualification.

author: @gazsto6733
text: I am currently in the middle of that process Robert and the certification and testing is not cheap and if it fails, you would need to redesign and implement the support advise provided by the product testing center to help the certification that's also changing from CE to UKCA (United Kingdom Conformity Assessed) from Jan 2023. Electrical Safety Testing & Assessments to Harmonised Standards - Undertake risk assessments as required by the Directive (and electrical safety standards). There are over 20 pieces of CE marking Legislation (Directives & Regulations) and if one or more applies to your product, then it is a legal requirement (in most cases) to CE (UKCA/Jan2023) mark it.

author: @Forever-into-Cars-and-Stars
text: Once a long long time ago. I had worked for a international Jewelry company. And as I learned my skills in sales and product knowledge. I questioned what if I have of a customer who wants to design there own ring? It was then when my mentor reached down and set on the countertop a book about the size of the Gutenberg Bible only three times as thick. Then he said this. They have been working gold sence Adam and Eve. And here's the record of it. Products catalogue of Simon & Golab. If you need more consult the Deluvian race records.

author: @stabarinde
text: Rob, just had another idea for the graphene ink - we've got a problem with interference at work, from neighbouring companies' 2.4GHz wireless equipment, it's seriously mucking up our work WiFi. Our guys have added a 5GHz router but most of our devices only have the 2.4GHz transceivers for 802.11n. So I was thinking about low impact shielding. If one coated a soft pliable mesh, like cotton fishnet, or silicone baking mesh with graphene paint it would easily shield wavelengths of 125mm, & could be laid easily under carpet tiles (our interfering neighbour is in the floor below). you could also stick it to walls & paper over, but for that you may need to use mesh-moulded casein as it's so thin... anyway, wasn't sure where to post this, but what do you think?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I think it's a good idea mate - I have just got the results back from a shielding effectiveness test that i sent off for - the results are very encouraging we get between  18 and 60dB attenuation at 4 microns from 20 Hz to 1.8GHz we didn't test up to 2.4GHz

replies.author: @stabarinde
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith ooh, cool, what substrate did you use? The 2.4GHz band might be a good one to aim for as there are so many devices using it now. Good luck!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
stabarinde
my  mistake - we went up to 18GHz not 1.8 - i need to read the zeros lol
